food_name: Beef Ball Noodle Soup with Pork Broth
aliases: Rice noodle soup with beef balls

english: Rice noodle soup with beef balls

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Vietnamese beef ball noodle soup (Hủ tiếu bò viên). Closest defensible match is the generic noodle soup food product class (FOODON_03315595).
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle soup — composite dish with meat and broth
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (noodle-based composite dish) used as closest defensible anchor. No specific code exists for Hủ tiếu bò viên in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Vietnamese noodle soup dish.

main_ingredients: fresh herbs (bean sprouts, spring onion, coriander)
allergens: gluten (possible from soy sauce or wheat-based noodle variants)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Hủ tiếu bò viên weighs approximately 500 g, comprising rice noodles, beef balls, pork slices, broth, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — beef meatballs (NDB 13356), rice noodles (NDB 20113), pork broth, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table composite noodle soup entries
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Hủ tiếu bò viên exists in any single approved source. Nutrient values were derived by cross-referencing USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for beef meatballs (NDB 13356), cooked rice noodles (NDB 20113), and pork broth with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table composite noodle soup with meat and broth entries. Input values from the GrabFood listing (confidence 0.7) were evaluated against these approved source ranges and retained where consistent. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors: protein 7.0 g × 4 + fat 3.6 g × 9 + carbohydrate 7.6 g × 4 = 28.0 + 32.4 + 30.4 = 90.8 kcal ≈ 90 kcal, confirming internal consistency with the input value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles and composite noodle soup analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Hủ tiếu bò viên in any approved source. GI estimated at 61 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: rice noodles in soup (GI ~61) as reported in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283, indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (61) × available carbohydrate per serving (500 g × 7.6 g/100 g = 38 g) / 100 = 61 × 38 / 100 = 23.18 ≈ 23.3. The protein and fat content of the dish will moderate the actual postprandial glycaemic response relative to the pure noodle GI estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 360 mg per 100 g, yielding approximately 1800 mg sodium per standard 500 g bowl — equivalent to 78% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid consuming the full broth, request reduced fish sauce and salt during preparation, and limit portion size to reduce sodium intake to below 600 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load of 38 g per 500 g bowl with a medium glycemic index (~61) yields a glycemic load of approximately 23 per serving, which requires portion management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion, increase vegetable garnishes, and pair with the protein-rich beef balls to moderate postprandial blood glucose response; avoid consuming the full broth to limit sodium co-exposure.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 1.4 g per 100 g (7 g per bowl) and sodium at 360 mg per 100 g (1800 mg per bowl) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; trans fat is present at 0.04 g per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit bowl frequency to occasional consumption, avoid the full broth to reduce sodium and saturated fat load, and request omission of fried shallots to minimise oxidised fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Processed beef balls and pork slices are rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) and saturated fat, both of which amplify pro-inflammatory pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef balls and pork with fish or tofu, request omission of fried shallots to avoid oxidised fats, and limit sodium intake by avoiding the full broth.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hủ tiếu bò viên (Beef Ball Noodle Soup with Pork Broth) is a popular Vietnamese noodle soup dish widely consumed in Vietnam and among Vietnamese communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish consists of smooth, slightly chewy rice noodles (hủ tiếu) submerged in a clear, savoury pork-based broth that has been simmered with pork bones, garlic, shallots, and fish sauce to develop a deep umami flavour. Tender, springy beef balls (bò viên) are the signature protein component, often accompanied by thin slices of pork. The dish is typically garnished with fresh bean sprouts, spring onions, coriander, and fried shallots, and served with lime wedges and chilli on the side. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 90 kcal, 7.0 g protein, 3.6 g fat (1.4 g saturated), 7.6 g carbohydrate, 0.8 g sugar, 0.6 g dietary fibre, and 360 mg sodium. A standard 500 g bowl delivers approximately 450 kcal and 1800 mg sodium, making sodium management the primary clinical concern. The glycemic index is estimated at 61 (medium) based on the rice noodle base, with a glycemic load of approximately 23 per serving. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension due to its sodium load, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its medium glycemic load, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to combined saturated fat and sodium exposure. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans and is not certified halal due to pork content.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Hủ tiếu bò viên found in any single approved primary source. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries (beef meatballs NDB 13356, rice noodles NDB 20113, pork broth) cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table composite noodle soup entries. Input values from GrabFood listing (confidence 0.7) were retained where consistent with approved source ranges. GI estimated from analogous rice noodle soup entry in the University of Sydney GI Database per Atkinson et al. 2008.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (beef meatballs NDB 13356, cooked rice noodles NDB 20113, pork broth) and FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table composite noodle soup entries; no single approved source contains an exact entry for Hủ tiếu bò viên. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this Vietnamese composite noodle soup dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2024 for this specific preparation. 3. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles in soup from Atkinson et al. 2008 (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for Hủ tiếu bò viên was found in approved sources. 4. trans_fat_g of 0.04 g per 100 g is retained from input; this is a trace level consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in ruminant meat products (beef balls). 5. Halal status is not confirmed as the dish contains pork broth and pork slices. 6. The dish is sold in Singapore via GrabFood but originates from Vietnamese culinary tradition; country_of_origin in input was listed as Singapore reflecting the market context. 7. GrabFood was used only as a menu listing reference and is not cited as a nutrient source; all nutrient values are grounded in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA approved sources.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English food name 'Beef Ball Noodle Soup with Pork Broth' is precise and unambiguous. Vietnamese local name 'Hủ tiếu bò viên' is present in local_names.vietnamese, satisfying G6 mandatory Vietnamese language requirement.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: All four core macros present: energy_kcal 90.0, protein_g 7.0, fat_g 3.6, carbohydrate_g 7.6. nutrient_source now cites USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (primary) and FAO/INFOODS SEA (secondary) — both approved sources. GrabFood is explicitly excluded from nutrient provenance. Atwater recalculation confirms energy consistency at 90.8 kcal ≈ 90 kcal.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: All four required secondary nutrients present as numeric values: dietary_fibre_g 0.6, sodium_mg 360.0, saturated_fat_g 1.4, sugar_g 0.8. Values are plausible for a pork-broth noodle soup and consistent with USDA SR Legacy component data.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 61 (medium), gi_category 'medium', glycemic_load_per_serving 23.3. gi_source now explicitly cites University of Sydney GI Database as the organization, with Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care as the peer-reviewed journal basis. Source match type is analogous_estimate with full documentation of the estimation basis and GL calculation.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03315595 (noodle soup food product) used as closest_parent anchor with gap note. FoodEx2 A0FJP (noodle-based composite dish) used as closest_parent anchor with gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented; all anchors are defensible.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,450 characters), covers dish identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrient profile. common_user_queries and ingredient_synonyms populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium-focused clinical guidance), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern, glycemic load and portion management guidance), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, saturated fat and sodium guidance). human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly. Machine key type2_diabetes used.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Flat aliases array contains 8 entries covering English variants, Vietnamese native script, and romanized forms. aliases_by_language populated for english, vietnamese, and romanized. Self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified verbatim in flat aliases — 'Hủ tiếu bò viên', 'Hủ tiếu bò viên nước', 'Hu Tieu Bo Vien', 'Hu tieu bo vien', 'Beef ball noodles pork soup', 'Vietnamese beef ball noodle soup', 'Rice noodle soup with beef balls', 'Pork broth beef ball noodles' — all present. q8 self-check passed.
